name: Chest Pain
summary: Undifferentiated chest pain; rule out ACS, PE, aortic dissection, pneumothorax, esophageal/musculoskeletal causes.

triage: Immediate ECG within 10 min of arrival for any anginal-equivalent symptom
Vitals incl. bilateral BP and SpO2
Brief OPQRST + cardiac risk factors (HTN, DM, smoking, family hx, prior CAD)
Flag diaphoresis, radiation, dyspnea, or hemodynamic instability for ESI 1-2
nursing: Place on continuous cardiac monitor and pulse oximetry
Establish 2 large-bore IVs (18g)
Draw initial troponin and cardiac labs with IV placement
Reassess pain score and vitals q15-30 min
Have defibrillator/crash cart accessible
keyOrders: 12-lead ECG STAT (repeat at 15-30 min if ongoing pain or initial nondiagnostic)
O2 only if SpO2 <90% (avoid routine hyperoxia)
Activate STEMI/transfer protocol if ST elevation*
labs: D-dimer if low PE pretest probability*
imaging: Portable CXR
CT pulmonary angiogram if PE suspected*
CT aorta if dissection suspected*
Bedside echo for wall-motion/effusion if available*
meds: Aspirin 324 mg PO chewed (non-enteric) unless contraindicated
Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL q5 min x3 PRN (hold if SBP <90, RV infarct, or PDE5 inhibitor use)
Morphine 2-4 mg IV PRN refractory pain (use judiciously)
Heparin per ACS protocol if NSTEMI/STEMI and transfer accepted*
Ondansetron 4 mg IV PRN nausea*
reassess: Repeat ECG with any pain change or at 15-30 min intervals
Trend serial troponins and HEART score
Reassess response to nitroglycerin and hemodynamics
Escalate immediately for dynamic ECG changes or rising troponin
disposition: STEMI or unstable: emergent transfer to PCI-capable center
NSTEMI/high HEART: admit-equivalent transfer to cardiology
Low HEART (0-3) with negative serial troponin: discharge with follow-up
Observation for indeterminate risk pending serial markers
discharge: Return precautions for recurrent or worsening chest pain, dyspnea, syncope
Strict instruction to call 911 for recurrent symptoms
Provide copy of ECG and labs for follow-up provider
Address modifiable risk factors and smoking cessation
followup: Cardiology or PCP within 24-72 h for low-risk discharge
Outpatient stress test or CT coronary angiography if indicated*
Medication reconciliation and statin/antiplatelet review

redFlags: ST-segment elevation or new LBBB on ECG
Hemodynamic instability, hypotension, or cardiogenic shock
Rising or markedly elevated troponin
Tearing/radiating-to-back pain or BP differential suggesting dissection
Hypoxia, syncope, or signs of massive PE
compliance: EMTALA medical screening exam with documented serial ECGs/troponins before disposition
STEMI/high-risk ACS requires stabilization and appropriate transfer to PCI-capable facility with physician-to-physician acceptance and signed transfer form
Document risks/benefits and patient consent for transfer; transfer by ALS with monitor
Texas FSED must arrange transfer agreement and ensure stabilizing treatment per EMTALA before moving patient

name: Palpitations
summary: Sensation of irregular/rapid heartbeat; differentiate benign ectopy from sustained arrhythmia, electrolyte, thyroid, or structural cause.

triage: 12-lead ECG to capture rhythm during symptoms
Vitals incl. HR, BP, SpO2
Screen for associated syncope, chest pain, dyspnea (upgrade acuity)
Ask about caffeine, stimulants, drugs, thyroid history
nursing: Place on continuous cardiac monitor
Establish IV access
Print rhythm strip during palpitation episodes
Reassess vitals and symptoms q30 min
keyOrders: Rhythm strip capture during symptomatic episode
Orthostatic vitals if volume depletion suspected*
labs: Troponin if chest pain or sustained tachyarrhythmia*
Urine/serum tox or beta-hCG if indicated*
imaging: CXR if cardiopulmonary symptoms*
Bedside echo if structural disease or HF suspected*
meds: Replete potassium (KCl 20-40 mEq PO/IV) and magnesium (2 g IV) for deficits
Metoprolol 25-50 mg PO or 5 mg IV for symptomatic rate control*
Treat underlying cause (anxiolytic, antipyretic, volume) as appropriate*
Refer arrhythmia-specific meds to relevant protocol (SVT/AF)*
reassess: Correlate symptoms with monitor/ECG findings
Recheck electrolytes after repletion
Reassess for sustained vs self-terminating arrhythmia
disposition: Sustained or unstable arrhythmia: transfer per Tachycardia/Bradycardia protocol
Benign ectopy with normal ECG/labs: discharge
Structural disease or recurrent syncope: cardiology referral/transfer
Observation if intermittent symptoms requiring monitoring
discharge: Return precautions for syncope, chest pain, sustained racing, dyspnea
Limit caffeine, stimulants, alcohol
Provide ECG copy for follow-up
followup: Cardiology for ambulatory monitor (Holter/event recorder)*
PCP for thyroid and electrolyte management
Echocardiogram outpatient if structural concern*

redFlags: Palpitations with syncope or near-syncope
Sustained wide-complex tachycardia on ECG
Hemodynamic instability or chest pain
Family history of sudden cardiac death or known structural disease
Markedly abnormal potassium or magnesium
compliance: EMTALA screening with rhythm documentation before discharge
Sustained dangerous arrhythmia requires stabilization and appropriate transfer with monitor and physician acceptance
Observation-vs-transfer based on rhythm stability and structural risk
Document captured rhythm strips in transfer packet when escalating

name: Hypertensive Urgency/Emergency
summary: Severe BP elevation (>=180/120); distinguish urgency (no end-organ damage) from emergency (acute target-organ injury).

triage: Confirm BP in both arms with correct cuff size
Rapid screen for end-organ symptoms: chest pain, dyspnea, neuro deficit, vision change
12-lead ECG and vitals incl. SpO2
Upgrade to ESI 1-2 if any acute end-organ findings
nursing: Continuous cardiac monitor and frequent automated BP cycling
Establish IV access
Place in quiet setting and recheck BP after rest for urgency
Neuro checks if any CNS symptoms
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor and serial BP (manual confirmation)
Arterial line if titratable IV therapy needed*
STAT neuro imaging if stroke symptoms*
labs: CBC with smear if MAHA suspected*
imaging: CXR if dyspnea/HF suspected
Non-contrast head CT for neuro symptoms*
CT aorta if dissection suspected*
meds: Emergency: labetalol 10-20 mg IV q10 min or infusion; or nicardipine 5 mg/h IV titrated
Aortic dissection: esmolol/labetalol to HR <60 then nicardipine (beta-block first)
Pulmonary edema: nitroglycerin IV infusion + furosemide 40 mg IV*
Urgency: resume/adjust home oral agents (e.g., amlodipine, lisinopril); avoid rapid drops
Target ~10-20% MAP reduction in first hour for emergencies (except stroke/dissection protocols)
reassess: Recheck BP q5-15 min during IV titration
Avoid overcorrection (>25% MAP drop in first hour)
Reassess end-organ symptoms continuously
Trend renal function and neuro status
disposition: Hypertensive emergency: transfer to ICU-capable facility for IV titration
Urgency without end-organ damage: discharge with oral therapy and close follow-up
Observation if borderline or awaiting BP response
Pregnancy-related severe HTN: emergent OB transfer
discharge: Return precautions for chest pain, neuro deficit, vision change, dyspnea
Provide adjusted oral antihypertensive plan
Avoid abrupt cessation of home meds
Home BP monitoring instructions
followup: PCP within 24-72 h for BP recheck and titration
Nephrology/cardiology if secondary or resistant HTN*
OB urgent follow-up for pregnancy-related HTN*

redFlags: Chest pain/ischemic ECG changes (ACS or dissection)
Focal neuro deficit, severe headache, or altered mental status
Acute pulmonary edema or dyspnea
Acute kidney injury, hematuria, or MAHA
Pregnancy >20 weeks with severe HTN (preeclampsia/eclampsia)
compliance: EMTALA screening to identify end-organ damage before disposition
Hypertensive emergency requires stabilization (initiate IV control) and appropriate ICU-level transfer with monitor
Document differentiation of urgency vs emergency to justify observation-vs-transfer
Preeclampsia/eclampsia mandates emergent OB-capable transfer; coordinate magnesium and acceptance

name: Syncope & Near-Syncope
summary: Transient loss of consciousness; differentiate benign vasovagal/orthostatic from cardiac, neurologic, or hemorrhagic causes.

triage: 12-lead ECG immediately to screen for arrhythmia/ischemia
Vitals with orthostatic BP/HR
Identify high-risk features (exertional, no prodrome, palpitations, family SCD)
Check glucose and screen for trauma from fall
nursing: Continuous cardiac monitor
IV access
Orthostatic vital signs
Reassess mental status and recurrent symptoms
imaging: CXR if cardiopulmonary cause suspected*
Non-contrast head CT if head trauma or neuro deficit*
CT/US aorta or abdomen if AAA/dissection suspected*
Bedside echo for structural disease*
meds: IV crystalloid bolus (NS 500-1000 mL) for orthostatic/volume depletion*
Treat underlying cause (rate/rhythm control, transfusion) per relevant protocol*
Generally no specific drug for vasovagal syncope
Antiemetic PRN nausea*
reassess: Apply risk score (Canadian Syncope Risk Score)
Recheck orthostatics after fluids
Monitor telemetry for arrhythmia during stay
Reassess for recurrent episodes or new symptoms
disposition: Cardiac/high-risk syncope: transfer for telemetry and cardiology workup
Low-risk vasovagal/orthostatic with normal ECG: discharge
Observation for intermediate-risk pending monitoring
Emergent transfer for suspected hemorrhage/dissection
discharge: Return precautions for recurrent syncope, chest pain, palpitations, exertional symptoms
Driving and activity precautions until cleared
Hydration and slow positional changes for vasovagal
Provide ECG copy
followup: Cardiology for high/intermediate risk (monitor, echo, EP)*
PCP for medication review and orthostatic management
Neurology if seizure suspected*

redFlags: Exertional syncope or syncope without prodrome
Abnormal ECG (arrhythmia, ischemia, long QT, Brugada, WPW)
Palpitations preceding event or family history of sudden death
Signs of GI bleed, AAA, ectopic pregnancy, or dissection
Persistent abnormal vitals or hypotension
compliance: EMTALA screening including ECG and risk stratification before disposition
High-risk cardiac syncope requires stabilization and appropriate transfer with telemetry
Document risk score to justify observation-vs-transfer decision
If syncope from elder abuse/neglect-related injury suspected, mandatory APS reporting in Texas

name: Bradycardia
summary: HR <50 with assessment for symptomatic high-grade block, ischemia, drug effect, or electrolyte/metabolic cause per AHA ACLS.

triage: 12-lead ECG immediately to define block degree
Vitals incl. BP, SpO2, mental status
Identify instability: hypotension, altered mental status, chest pain, shock
Medication review (beta-blocker, CCB, digoxin) and recent ingestions
nursing: Continuous cardiac monitor and transcutaneous pacing pads on patient
Establish IV access x2
Prepare atropine and pacing/pressor infusions
Frequent vital sign and perfusion checks
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor with pacer pads applied
Prepare transcutaneous pacing for unstable patient
imaging: CXR
Bedside echo if structural/ischemic cause suspected*
meds: Atropine 1 mg IV q3-5 min (max 3 mg) for unstable bradycardia
Dopamine infusion 5-20 mcg/kg/min or epinephrine 2-10 mcg/min if atropine fails
Transcutaneous pacing for refractory/high-grade block (sedate with care)
Calcium/glucagon/high-dose insulin for CCB or beta-blocker toxicity*
Treat hyperkalemia (calcium gluconate, insulin/dextrose) if present*
reassess: Continuous reassessment of perfusion and BP after each intervention
Confirm electrical and mechanical capture if pacing
Trend electrolytes and repeat ECG
Escalate to transvenous pacing planning if refractory
disposition: Unstable or high-grade AV block: emergent transfer for transvenous pacing/cardiology
Reversible cause corrected and asymptomatic: observation or discharge
Symptomatic stable: transfer for monitoring and pacemaker evaluation
Toxicologic causes: transfer to ICU with poison center consult
discharge: Only if benign, asymptomatic, reversible cause resolved
Return precautions for syncope, dizziness, chest pain, dyspnea
Medication adjustment instructions
Provide ECG copy
followup: Cardiology/EP for pacemaker evaluation*
PCP for medication and electrolyte management
Toxicology follow-up if ingestion*

redFlags: Hypotension, shock, or altered mental status with bradycardia
Mobitz II or third-degree (complete) heart block
Bradycardia with ischemic ECG changes
Failure to respond to atropine
Severe hyperkalemia or drug toxicity
compliance: EMTALA stabilization including atropine/pacing before transfer
Unstable bradycardia requires appropriate transfer to facility with transvenous pacing/EP capability and physician acceptance
Document stabilizing interventions and continuous monitoring during transfer
Suspected intentional overdose triggers behavioral health evaluation and applicable reporting

name: Tachycardia / SVT
summary: HR >100 narrow- or wide-complex tachycardia; classify per ACLS and treat per stability and rhythm (SVT, AF/flutter, VT).

triage: 12-lead ECG immediately to classify width/regularity
Vitals incl. BP, SpO2, mental status
Assess instability: hypotension, chest pain, dyspnea, altered mental status
Identify triggers (fever, dehydration, stimulants, hyperthyroid)
nursing: Continuous cardiac monitor with defibrillator/pads at bedside
IV access x2
Prepare adenosine and synchronized cardioversion setup
Continuous SpO2 and frequent BP
keyOrders: 12-lead ECG STAT (and during vagal/adenosine for diagnosis)
Prepare synchronized cardioversion for unstable patient
imaging: CXR if HF or pulmonary cause suspected*
Bedside echo for function/effusion if unstable*
meds: Stable SVT: vagal maneuvers, then adenosine 6 mg rapid IV, then 12 mg
Stable AF/flutter RVR: diltiazem 0.25 mg/kg IV (~20 mg) or metoprolol 5 mg IV
Stable monomorphic VT: amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 min
Unstable any rhythm: synchronized cardioversion (50-200 J) with sedation
Torsades: magnesium 2 g IV; correct K/Mg deficits
reassess: Recheck rhythm and vitals after each intervention
Repeat 12-lead post-conversion
Trend electrolytes and reassess for recurrence
Escalate to cardioversion if deterioration
disposition: Unstable or VT: transfer for cardiology/ICU after stabilization
New AF/flutter or refractory SVT: transfer or cardiology evaluation
Resolved SVT with normal ECG and stable: discharge
Observation for rate control titration
discharge: Return precautions for recurrence, syncope, chest pain, dyspnea
Avoid stimulants/caffeine; teach vagal maneuvers for SVT
Anticoagulation counseling if new AF (per CHA2DS2-VASc)*
Provide ECG copy
followup: Cardiology/EP for ablation evaluation in recurrent SVT*
Cardiology for new AF rate/rhythm and anticoagulation*
PCP for thyroid/electrolyte management

redFlags: Hypotension, chest pain, dyspnea, or altered mental status with tachycardia
Wide-complex tachycardia (assume VT until proven otherwise)
Signs of heart failure or shock
New AF with rapid rate and instability
Failure to respond to first-line therapy
compliance: EMTALA stabilization (cardioversion/pharmacologic control) before transfer
Unstable arrhythmia requires appropriate transfer with monitor and physician acceptance
Document rhythm diagnosis and interventions in transfer packet
Observation-vs-transfer based on rhythm conversion and recurrence risk

name: Cardiac Arrest
summary: Pulseless arrest requiring immediate ACLS; identify and treat reversible Hs and Ts; manage post-ROSC for transfer.

triage: Confirm unresponsiveness, no pulse, no normal breathing
Activate code/ACLS team and call for defibrillator
Note time of arrest and witnessed vs unwitnessed
Highest acuity ESI 1
nursing: Begin high-quality CPR (100-120/min, minimize interruptions)
Apply defibrillator pads and rhythm-check q2 min
Establish IV/IO access x2
Assign roles: compressions, airway, meds, timekeeper/recorder
Prepare and document medications and shock times
keyOrders: Continuous CPR with rhythm checks q2 min
Advanced airway (BVM then ETT/supraglottic) with capnography
labs: POC glucose, potassium, and iSTAT/VBG with electrolytes
imaging: Bedside echo during pulse checks for cardiac activity/tamponade/RV strain
CXR post-intubation to confirm tube placement
POCUS for pneumothorax/PE if suspected*
meds: Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO q3-5 min
Amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO (then 150 mg) for refractory VF/pVT (or lidocaine)
Calcium gluconate + insulin/dextrose for hyperkalemia*
Sodium bicarbonate for hyperkalemia/TCA toxicity*
Post-ROSC: norepinephrine/epinephrine infusion for hypotension; targeted O2/ventilation
reassess: Rhythm and pulse check q2 min; reassess CPR quality and capnography
Systematically address Hs and Ts each cycle
On ROSC: 12-lead ECG, support BP, secure airway
Consider termination per protocol if prolonged asystole and no reversible cause
disposition: Post-ROSC: emergent transfer to PCI/ICU-capable facility with critical care transport
Activate cath lab transfer if STEMI on post-ROSC ECG
Targeted temperature management coordination at receiving facility
Pronounce per protocol if resuscitation unsuccessful and document
discharge: Not applicable; survivors require admission/transfer
Provide family updates and support
Coordinate organ donation discussion if applicable per protocol*
followup: Receiving cardiology/intensivist assumes care
Notify family/next of kin and document
Debrief code team

redFlags: Refractory VF/pVT despite defibrillation
PEA/asystole with unidentified reversible cause
Post-ROSC hypotension or recurrent arrest
STEMI on post-ROSC ECG
Signs of tamponade, tension pneumothorax, or massive PE
compliance: EMTALA requires full resuscitative stabilization regardless of payer status
Post-ROSC patients require appropriate transfer to PCI/ICU-capable center with critical-care transport and physician acceptance
Document code timeline, ROSC, and transfer paperwork thoroughly
Report to medical examiner for unexpected/unattended death; suspected homicide/overdose triggers mandatory reporting

name: Peripheral Edema
summary: Lower-extremity swelling; differentiate cardiac/renal/hepatic, venous insufficiency, DVT, lymphedema, and medication causes.

triage: Vitals incl. SpO2 and weight
Determine unilateral vs bilateral and acuity of onset
Screen for dyspnea, orthopnea, chest pain (cardiac) and DVT features
Upgrade if respiratory distress or suspected DVT
nursing: IV access if systemic illness suspected
Elevate affected extremities
Cardiac monitor if cardiopulmonary symptoms
Measure calf circumference if unilateral
keyOrders: Venous duplex order if unilateral DVT concern*
labs: LFTs and albumin if hepatic/nephrotic cause suspected*
imaging: Venous duplex ultrasound for unilateral edema (rule out DVT)*
CXR if HF/dyspnea*
Bedside echo if cardiac dysfunction suspected*
meds: Furosemide 20-40 mg IV/PO for volume overload/HF*
Continue/optimize HF or renal regimen per cause*
Adjust offending meds (CCB, NSAID) when feasible*
Compression therapy for chronic venous insufficiency*
reassess: Reassess oxygenation and respiratory status
Monitor diuretic response and renal function
Reassess for unilateral findings warranting DVT workup
Reassess vitals for systemic decompensation
disposition: Acute HF/pulmonary edema or AKI: transfer for admission-level care
Suspected DVT: manage per DVT protocol
Chronic stable bilateral edema: discharge with outpatient workup
Observation for diuresis response if borderline
discharge: Return precautions for dyspnea, chest pain, sudden unilateral swelling/pain
Leg elevation, compression, and sodium/fluid guidance
Medication reconciliation for offending agents
Weight monitoring instructions for HF
followup: PCP/cardiology for HF management and echo*
Nephrology/hepatology for renal/hepatic causes*
Vascular/wound clinic for venous insufficiency*

redFlags: Bilateral edema with dyspnea, orthopnea, or hypoxia (decompensated HF)
Unilateral painful swelling suggesting DVT
Acute kidney injury or anasarca
Signs of cellulitis/infection with systemic toxicity
New severe edema with chest pain
compliance: EMTALA screening to exclude life threats (HF, DVT, AKI) before discharge
Decompensated HF requires stabilization and appropriate transfer for admission
Document workup distinguishing emergent vs chronic to justify observation-vs-transfer
If edema reflects neglect in dependent/elderly patient, consider APS reporting

name: Suspected DVT
summary: Unilateral leg pain/swelling; risk-stratify (Wells), confirm with D-dimer/ultrasound, and assess for concurrent PE.

triage: Vitals incl. SpO2 and HR
Assess unilateral swelling, pain, warmth, calf tenderness
Screen for PE symptoms (dyspnea, chest pain, tachycardia) and upgrade
Identify risk factors (recent surgery, immobility, malignancy, prior VTE)
nursing: Measure and compare bilateral calf circumference
IV access if anticoagulation likely
Elevate affected limb
Cardiac monitor/SpO2 if PE concern
keyOrders: Venous duplex ultrasound of affected limb
12-lead ECG and SpO2 if PE suspected*
labs: CBC and BMP (baseline renal function for anticoagulation)
Troponin/BNP if PE with strain suspected*
imaging: Compression venous duplex ultrasound (confirmatory)
CT pulmonary angiogram if concurrent PE suspected*
Repeat ultrasound in 5-7 days if initial negative but high suspicion*
meds: Anticoagulation if DVT confirmed: rivaroxaban 15 mg PO BID or apixaban 10 mg PO BID
Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC q12h as alternative/bridge*
Heparin IV infusion if renal impairment or possible intervention*
Analgesia (acetaminophen 1 g PO) for pain*
reassess: Reassess for PE symptoms and oxygenation
Confirm renal function before/while anticoagulating
Reassess bleeding risk and contraindications
Arrange repeat imaging if deferred and high suspicion
disposition: Uncomplicated DVT, low bleeding risk: discharge on DOAC with follow-up
Phlegmasia, extensive iliofemoral, or concurrent PE: transfer for admission/intervention
Inability to arrange outpatient anticoagulation: observation or transfer
Negative ultrasound with low risk: discharge with return precautions
discharge: Return precautions for dyspnea, chest pain, worsening swelling, bleeding
Anticoagulant adherence, dosing, and bleeding-precaution education
Avoid prolonged immobility; ambulation guidance
Provide anticoagulation duration plan
followup: PCP/anticoagulation clinic within 3-7 days
Hematology if recurrent/unprovoked VTE or thrombophilia*
Malignancy workup if unprovoked clot*

redFlags: Concurrent PE features: dyspnea, hypoxia, tachycardia, chest pain, syncope
Phlegmasia cerulea dolens (massive iliofemoral thrombosis, limb-threatening)
Hemodynamic instability
Signs of active bleeding precluding anticoagulation
Rapidly progressive swelling or pulselessness
compliance: EMTALA screening to exclude PE/limb-threatening thrombosis before discharge
Limb-threatening or PE-associated DVT requires stabilization and appropriate transfer for intervention
Document Wells score, imaging, and anticoagulation plan
Ensure reliable outpatient anticoagulation access before discharge from FSED

name: Acute Limb Ischemia
summary: Sudden arterial occlusion threatening limb viability; recognize 6 Ps, anticoagulate, and arrange emergent vascular intervention.

triage: Immediate assessment of 6 Ps: pain, pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, poikilothermia, paralysis
Vitals and contralateral limb comparison
Time of symptom onset (revascularization window critical)
Triage as ESI 1-2; this is a limb emergency
nursing: Establish IV access x2
Continuous cardiac monitor (assess for AF as embolic source)
Keep limb dependent, warm room, do NOT apply heat or cold/elevate
Mark pulse locations; frequent neurovascular checks
keyOrders: Doppler arterial exam of affected limb
imaging: CT angiography of affected extremity (at receiving center if transferring)
Arterial duplex ultrasound if immediately available*
Do not delay anticoagulation/transfer for imaging
meds: Heparin IV bolus 80 units/kg then infusion 18 units/kg/h (unless contraindicated)
Analgesia: morphine 2-4 mg IV titrated for ischemic pain
IV fluids for hydration/renal protection
Antiemetic PRN*
reassess: Serial neurovascular and Doppler checks
Reassess limb viability category (Rutherford) and motor/sensory function
Monitor for compartment syndrome and reperfusion injury
Confirm therapeutic anticoagulation
disposition: Emergent transfer to vascular surgery-capable center for revascularization
Activate transfer immediately; time is limb
Coordinate possible thrombolysis/thrombectomy/embolectomy at receiving facility
No discharge from FSED for true ALI
discharge: Not applicable; ALI requires emergent vascular intervention
If symptoms fully resolve and ALI excluded, vascular follow-up with return precautions
Provide records and imaging to receiving team
followup: Vascular surgery assumes care post-revascularization
Cardiology for embolic source (AF) and anticoagulation*
Hematology if thrombophilia suspected*

redFlags: Absent pulses with sensory/motor deficit (advanced ischemia)
Paralysis, profound paresthesia, or muscle rigidity (nonviable limb)
Mottled, non-blanching skin
Signs of compartment syndrome or rhabdomyolysis
Delayed presentation beyond viability window
compliance: EMTALA requires stabilization (anticoagulation, analgesia) and emergent appropriate transfer to vascular-capable facility
Physician-to-physician acceptance and signed transfer documenting limb-threatening emergency
Do not delay transfer for FSED imaging that is unavailable; transfer is the stabilizing intervention
Document onset time, exam, and anticoagulation in transfer packet

name: Suspected Aortic Dissection
summary: Acute aortic syndrome with tearing pain; control HR/BP, confirm with CT angiography, and arrange emergent surgical transfer.

triage: Immediate ECG and bilateral BP measurement (pulse/BP differential)
Identify abrupt tearing/ripping chest or back pain, migrating pain
Screen for neuro deficit, syncope, malperfusion signs
Highest acuity ESI 1
nursing: Continuous cardiac monitor and bilateral/arterial BP
Establish 2 large-bore IVs
Type and crossmatch; prepare for possible massive transfusion
Frequent neuro and pulse checks across extremities
keyOrders: 12-lead ECG (assess for inferior MI from RCA involvement)
CT angiography chest/abdomen/pelvis STAT (or at receiving facility if transfer faster)
labs: D-dimer (adjunct; do not rule out solely on D-dimer)*
imaging: CT angiography of aorta (definitive) if available without delaying transfer
Transesophageal/transthoracic echo if unstable and CT not feasible*
CXR (widened mediastinum is supportive, not exclusionary)
meds: Esmolol infusion or labetalol 20 mg IV to target HR <60 FIRST (impulse control)
Then nicardipine/nitroprusside infusion to target SBP 100-120 mmHg
Analgesia: morphine/fentanyl IV for pain (aids BP control)
Avoid vasodilators before beta-blockade (prevents reflex tachycardia/shear)
Blood products for hypotension/rupture (avoid over-resuscitation)
reassess: Continuous BP/HR with goal SBP 100-120 and HR <60
Reassess for malperfusion (neuro, limb, mesenteric, renal)
Monitor for tamponade/hypotension (Type A complication)
Reassess pain and hemodynamics frequently
disposition: Type A: emergent transfer to cardiothoracic surgery center
Type B: transfer for medical management and vascular monitoring (ICU)
Activate transfer in parallel with stabilization; time-critical
No discharge from FSED for confirmed/strongly suspected dissection
discharge: Not applicable; confirmed/suspected dissection requires emergent transfer
If dissection definitively excluded, treat alternative diagnosis with return precautions
Provide imaging and records to receiving team
followup: Cardiothoracic/vascular surgery assumes care
Cardiology for long-term BP control and surveillance imaging*
Genetics referral if connective tissue disorder suspected*

redFlags: Pulse or BP differential between extremities
Hypotension, syncope, or signs of tamponade (Type A)
New neurologic deficit or malperfusion (stroke, limb, mesenteric, renal)
New aortic regurgitation murmur
Hemodynamic collapse suggesting rupture
compliance: EMTALA requires stabilization (impulse/BP control) and emergent appropriate transfer to surgical-capable center
Physician-to-physician acceptance and signed transfer documenting time-critical emergency
Do not delay transfer for CT if it is unavailable or slower than transport; coordinate imaging at receiving center
Document bilateral BP, anti-impulse therapy, and ongoing monitoring during ALS transfer

name: Rash
summary: Undifferentiated cutaneous eruption; triage benign exanthems from life-threatening rashes (SJS/TEN, meningococcemia, RMSF, anaphylaxis, necrotizing infection).

triage: Vital signs incl. temperature; flag fever + rash for rapid assessment
Screen for airway/mucosal involvement, hypotension, AMS (sepsis vs anaphylaxis)
Ask onset, spread pattern, new meds/foods, tick/travel exposure
Isolate immediately if petechial/purpuric rash + fever (possible meningococcemia)*
nursing: Photograph lesions for documentation per consent
Assess body surface area involved and mucous membranes (eyes, mouth, genitals)
Pain/itch scoring; place in private room if contagious concern
Establish IV access if systemic illness suspected* if indicated
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor & pulse oximetry if systemic signs* if indicated
IV access for ill-appearing or febrile patients* if indicated
Nikolsky sign and mucosal exam by physician
Strict isolation precautions for suspected meningococcemia* if indicated
labs: CMP, CRP for systemic/febrile presentations* if indicated
Blood cultures x2 if febrile/toxic* if indicated
Skin biopsy referral for unclear severe eruptions* if indicated
imaging: Generally none for uncomplicated rash
Soft-tissue US/CT if necrotizing infection suspected* if indicated
meds: Diphenhydramine 25-50 mg PO/IV for pruritus* if indicated
Triamcinolone 0.1% topical BID for localized inflammatory rash* if indicated
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO for fever* if indicated
Empiric ceftriaxone 2 g IV STAT if meningococcemia suspected* if indicated
reassess: Recheck vitals and rash progression within 30-60 min if systemic
Reassess airway/mucosa if any swelling or mucosal lesions
Confirm response to antihistamine/steroid before discharge
disposition: Discharge most benign localized exanthems
Admit/transfer febrile toxic-appearing patients or suspected SJS/TEN
Transfer to facility with burn/ICU & dermatology for blistering mucosal disease
discharge: Topical/oral therapy instructions and trigger avoidance
Return for spreading, blistering, mouth/eye involvement, fever, or breathing trouble
Stop suspected culprit medication if drug etiology likely
followup: Primary care or dermatology within 3-7 days
Sooner follow-up if worsening or systemic symptoms
Document culprit drug in allergy list if suspected

redFlags: Fever + petechiae/purpura (meningococcemia, RMSF, DIC)
Mucosal involvement, skin pain, or positive Nikolsky (SJS/TEN)
Hypotension, wheeze, or facial/airway swelling (anaphylaxis)
Rapidly spreading erythema with pain out of proportion (necrotizing fasciitis)
compliance: EMTALA medical screening exam to exclude SJS/TEN, meningococcemia, anaphylaxis, necrotizing infection before discharge
If high-acuity dermatologic emergency exceeds FSED capability, stabilize and arrange appropriate transfer with physician-to-physician acceptance and records
Report suspected meningococcal disease and other reportable communicable conditions to Texas DSHS
Document observation-vs-transfer rationale; FSED has no inpatient admission

name: Cellulitis
summary: Acute bacterial skin/soft-tissue infection (Strep/Staph); distinguish purulent vs nonpurulent and exclude abscess, DVT, and necrotizing infection.

triage: Vitals incl. temperature; flag SIRS criteria for sepsis pathway
Mark borders of erythema with skin marker and time
Screen risk factors: diabetes, immunosuppression, IVDU, lymphedema, PVD
Assess for pain out of proportion or crepitus (necrotizing concern)*
nursing: Elevate and immobilize affected limb
Measure and document area; photograph if consented
Pain assessment and analgesia per protocol
Obtain wound/purulent culture if drainage present* if indicated
keyOrders: IV access for systemic infection or IV antibiotics* if indicated
Mark erythema margins to track progression
Bedside US to evaluate for occult abscess or fluid collection
labs: CMP and lactate if systemic/septic* if indicated
CRP/ESR for severity assessment* if indicated
Blood cultures x2 if febrile/toxic or immunocompromised* if indicated
imaging: Bedside soft-tissue ultrasound for abscess vs phlegmon
Venous duplex if DVT cannot be excluded* if indicated
CT/MRI if necrotizing infection or deep abscess suspected* if indicated
meds: Nonpurulent outpatient: cephalexin 500 mg PO QID or clindamycin 450 mg PO TID
Purulent/MRSA risk: TMP-SMX DS 1-2 tabs PO BID or doxycycline 100 mg PO BID
Severe/IV: cefazolin 1-2 g IV q8h or vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV for MRSA coverage
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO and analgesia for pain/fever* if indicated
reassess: Recheck vitals and erythema borders after IV fluids/antibiotics
Confirm no progression beyond marked margins before discharge
Reassess pain control and ability to tolerate oral meds
disposition: Discharge mild-moderate nonseptic cellulitis on oral antibiotics
Observation/IV antibiotics for moderate cases failing oral or with comorbidities
Transfer septic, necrotizing, or immunocompromised patients needing admission
discharge: Complete full oral antibiotic course; elevate limb
Return if fever, spreading redness past marked line, blistering, or increasing pain
Strict return precautions for diabetics and immunosuppressed
followup: Primary care recheck in 48-72 hours to confirm improvement
Earlier follow-up if not improving on oral therapy
Address underlying lymphedema/tinea/venous insufficiency to prevent recurrence

redFlags: Pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, or skin necrosis (necrotizing fasciitis)
Sepsis: hypotension, tachycardia, altered mentation, elevated lactate
Rapid progression despite appropriate antibiotics
Periorbital/orbital or facial involvement near airway
compliance: EMTALA MSE to exclude necrotizing soft-tissue infection and sepsis before any disposition
Necrotizing infection requires emergent surgical consult; stabilize and transfer to facility with surgery/ICU via appropriate-transfer protocol
Document observation-vs-transfer decision; FSED cannot admit, so escalate septic patients
Provide stabilizing IV antibiotics and fluids prior to transfer of unstable patients

name: Cutaneous Abscess
summary: Localized purulent collection requiring incision & drainage; consider MRSA, and evaluate special sites (perianal, facial, breast, hand).

triage: Vitals incl. temperature; flag fever/SIRS
Assess size, location, fluctuance, and surrounding cellulitis
Identify high-risk sites: face/central triangle, perianal, hand, genital
Screen diabetes/immunosuppression and IVDU
nursing: Position and expose lesion; prepare I&D tray and sterile field
Pain assessment; offer analgesia before procedure
Set up local anesthetic and wound culture swab
Document size and photograph if consented
keyOrders: Bedside ultrasound to confirm drainable collection vs phlegmon
Incision & drainage with loop or wick packing as indicated
Wound culture of purulent material (MRSA prevalence)
IV access if systemic signs* if indicated
labs: Usually none for simple abscess in healthy adult
CBC, CMP, lactate if febrile/toxic or immunocompromised* if indicated
Blood glucose in diabetics* if indicated
Blood cultures if systemic infection* if indicated
imaging: Bedside soft-tissue US to map collection and guide drainage
CT for deep, perirectal, or complex/recurrent abscess* if indicated
meds: Local anesthesia: lidocaine 1% with/without epinephrine for I&D
Adjunctive MRSA antibiotics if cellulitis/comorbidity: TMP-SMX DS BID or doxycycline 100 mg BID
Clindamycin 450 mg PO TID as alternative* if indicated
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for post-procedure analgesia* if indicated
reassess: Confirm adequate drainage and hemostasis post-I&D
Reassess pain and surrounding erythema
Verify patient/caregiver can perform wound/packing care
disposition: Discharge most simple abscesses after I&D
Observation/IV antibiotics if extensive cellulitis or systemic illness
Transfer/surgery referral for perirectal, complex, or deep abscesses
discharge: Wound care, packing/wick management, and dressing changes
Take adjunctive antibiotics if prescribed; warm compresses
Return for fever, spreading redness, increasing pain, or re-accumulation
followup: Wound recheck/packing removal in 48 hours
Primary care or surgery follow-up for recurrent abscesses (consider hidradenitis)
MRSA decolonization counseling for recurrent cases* if indicated

redFlags: Systemic toxicity/sepsis with abscess
Facial central-triangle abscess (cavernous sinus risk)
Crepitus, dusky skin, or pain out of proportion (necrotizing process)
Perirectal/deep abscess requiring operative drainage
compliance: EMTALA MSE before procedure; exclude necrotizing infection and sepsis
Complex/deep abscesses beyond FSED scope require stabilization and appropriate transfer to surgical facility
Document consent for I&D and observation-vs-transfer rationale
Report cluster/recurrent MRSA per facility infection-control policy

name: Urticaria / Hives
summary: Pruritic transient wheals from mast-cell activation; critical to distinguish isolated urticaria from evolving anaphylaxis or angioedema.

triage: Vitals incl. SpO2; immediate assessment for airway/breathing compromise
Screen for angioedema, throat tightness, wheeze, GI symptoms, hypotension (anaphylaxis)
Identify trigger: food, drug, insect sting, infection, contact
Escalate to ESI 1-2 if any systemic/respiratory involvement
nursing: Place on monitor if any systemic features
Establish IV access if anaphylaxis suspected* if indicated
Have epinephrine and airway equipment ready for moderate-severe cases
Document distribution and any mucosal/lip/tongue swelling
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor & pulse oximetry if systemic symptoms* if indicated
IV access and O2 for anaphylaxis* if indicated
Remove/stop suspected trigger (e.g., stop offending drug)
labs: None required for simple acute urticaria
Consider workup only for chronic (>6 wk) urticaria via outpatient
Tryptase if anaphylaxis/mastocytosis suspected* if indicated
imaging: Not indicated for urticaria
Soft-tissue imaging only if alternative diagnosis considered* if indicated
meds: Cetirizine 10 mg PO or diphenhydramine 25-50 mg PO/IV (H1 blocker)
Famotidine 20 mg IV/PO (H2 blocker) as adjunct
Prednisone 40-60 mg PO for moderate/refractory cases
Epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg IM (1 mg/mL) STAT if anaphylaxis/angioedema with airway* if indicated
reassess: Recheck airway, vitals, and lesion response after antihistamine
Observe 1-2 hours; longer if epinephrine given (biphasic risk)
Confirm resolution of any respiratory/GI symptoms before discharge
disposition: Discharge isolated urticaria responsive to antihistamines
Observe post-epinephrine cases per biphasic monitoring
Transfer for ICU-level airway management if severe angioedema/anaphylaxis
discharge: Continue scheduled non-sedating antihistamine x several days; short steroid taper if prescribed
Strict trigger avoidance; prescribe epinephrine auto-injector if anaphylaxis* if indicated
Return immediately for throat tightness, swelling, wheeze, or dizziness
followup: Primary care follow-up; allergy referral for recurrent or anaphylactic episodes
Document trigger/allergy in chart and counsel on auto-injector use
Dermatology/allergy for chronic urticaria >6 weeks

redFlags: Lip/tongue/throat swelling or stridor (airway angioedema)
Wheeze, hypoxia, hypotension, or syncope (anaphylaxis)
GI symptoms (vomiting, cramping) with urticaria suggesting systemic reaction
ACE-inhibitor angioedema unresponsive to antihistamines
compliance: EMTALA MSE to exclude anaphylaxis/angioedema; treat airway as emergency before any transfer
Stabilize with epinephrine/airway support; transfer for ICU airway needs via appropriate-transfer protocol
Document biphasic observation period and disposition rationale
Report suspected drug reaction to allergy record; no mandatory state report for isolated urticaria

name: Herpes Zoster
summary: Dermatomal vesicular eruption from VZV reactivation; identify ophthalmic involvement, disseminated disease, and immunocompromise.

triage: Vitals; assess pain severity and dermatomal distribution
Screen for ophthalmic (V1) involvement and Hutchinson sign
Identify immunocompromised status and disseminated lesions
Place airborne/contact precautions if disseminated or immunocompromised*
nursing: Isolate from pregnant/immunocompromised staff and patients
Cover lesions with dry dressing; document dermatome and crusting
Pain assessment and early analgesia
Photograph rash if consented
keyOrders: Physician exam of eye if facial/V1 distribution (slit-lamp/fluorescein)
Contact precautions; airborne if disseminated or immunocompromised
IV access for IV antiviral if severe/disseminated* if indicated
labs: Usually clinical diagnosis; no routine labs
VZV PCR of lesion if diagnosis unclear* if indicated
HIV testing if unexplained zoster in younger patient* if indicated
CBC/CMP if disseminated or systemically ill* if indicated
imaging: Not routinely indicated
Imaging only if CNS involvement or complications suspected* if indicated
meds: Valacyclovir 1000 mg PO TID x7 days (start within 72h or while new lesions forming)
Acyclovir 800 mg PO 5x/day as alternative
IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h for ophthalmic, disseminated, or immunocompromised* if indicated
Analgesia: acetaminophen, NSAIDs, gabapentin 300 mg PO; opioids for severe pain* if indicated
reassess: Reassess pain control adequacy before discharge
Confirm eye involvement addressed/ophthalmology arranged if V1
Verify no signs of dissemination
disposition: Discharge uncomplicated localized zoster on oral antivirals
Transfer/admit for disseminated zoster or severe immunocompromise needing IV therapy
Urgent ophthalmology for herpes zoster ophthalmicus
discharge: Complete antiviral course; keep lesions covered until crusted
Avoid contact with pregnant women, newborns, and immunocompromised until crusted
Return for eye symptoms, spreading rash, confusion, or uncontrolled pain
followup: Primary care follow-up; ophthalmology within 24-48h if ophthalmic
Counsel on postherpetic neuralgia and recombinant zoster vaccine when recovered
Pain management referral if persistent neuralgia

redFlags: Ophthalmic involvement / Hutchinson sign (vision-threatening)
Disseminated zoster (>2 dermatomes or crossing midline)
Immunocompromised host with rapidly progressing lesions
Signs of CNS involvement: meningismus, encephalopathy, focal deficit
compliance: EMTALA MSE to identify ophthalmic/disseminated/CNS disease requiring higher level of care
Transfer immunocompromised or disseminated cases needing IV antivirals/admission via appropriate-transfer protocol
Implement airborne+contact precautions for disseminated or immunocompromised patients
No mandatory state reporting for routine zoster; document infection-control measures

name: Drug Eruption
summary: Cutaneous adverse drug reaction spanning benign morbilliform to severe SCARs (SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP); risk stratify and stop the culprit.

triage: Vitals incl. temperature; flag fever + rash as severe-reaction warning
Detailed medication timeline (new drugs 1-8 weeks prior)
Assess mucosal involvement, skin pain, blistering, facial edema
Escalate ESI 1-2 if mucosal/blistering/systemic features
nursing: Stop all suspected culprit medications immediately
Examine and document mucous membranes, BSA, Nikolsky sign
IV access for ill/severe presentations* if indicated
Photograph rash and map extent if consented
keyOrders: Physician assessment for SCAR features (mucosal, Nikolsky, lymphadenopathy)
Discontinue offending agent and document in allergy record
IV access and monitor if systemic involvement* if indicated
Calculate SCORTEN if SJS/TEN suspected* if indicated
labs: CBC with differential (eosinophilia/atypical lymphocytes suggests DRESS)* if indicated
CMP and LFTs for organ involvement (DRESS)* if indicated
CRP/ESR; consider blood cultures if febrile/toxic* if indicated
Skin biopsy referral for severe/blistering eruptions* if indicated
imaging: Not routinely indicated
Chest imaging if DRESS with pulmonary symptoms* if indicated
meds: Stop culprit drug (primary intervention)
Diphenhydramine 25-50 mg PO/IV and cetirizine 10 mg PO for pruritus
Topical triamcinolone 0.1% for mild morbilliform rash* if indicated
Systemic corticosteroids only per specialist for DRESS/severe reactions* if indicated
reassess: Recheck vitals and rash progression; reassess mucosa
Confirm culprit drug stopped and documented
Evaluate for evolving SCAR features before discharge
disposition: Discharge benign morbilliform eruption after stopping culprit
Transfer SJS/TEN to burn/ICU center; admit/transfer DRESS for monitoring
Observation if borderline pending lab/specialist input
discharge: Permanently avoid culprit drug and chemically related agents
Antihistamines and topical steroid for symptom control
Return urgently for blistering, mouth/eye/genital sores, skin pain, or fever
followup: Primary care and dermatology/allergy follow-up
Document drug allergy prominently; provide written list to patient
Allergy referral for confirmatory evaluation if needed

redFlags: Mucosal erosions, skin pain, blistering, positive Nikolsky (SJS/TEN)
Fever, facial edema, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia, organ involvement (DRESS)
Sterile pustules with fever (AGEP)
Rapidly progressive skin detachment or systemic instability
compliance: EMTALA MSE to identify SCARs (SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP) before disposition
SJS/TEN beyond FSED scope: stabilize fluids/skin/airway and transfer to burn/ICU center via appropriate-transfer protocol
Document culprit drug and report serious adverse drug reaction to FDA MedWatch
Record observation-vs-transfer rationale; FSED cannot admit for monitoring

name: Wound Infection
summary: Local/systemic infection of traumatic or surgical wound; assess for retained foreign body, abscess, necrotizing infection, tetanus risk, and sepsis.

triage: Vitals incl. temperature; flag SIRS/sepsis
Mechanism, age of wound, contamination (bite, water, soil, retained object)
Tetanus immunization history
Assess for crepitus, necrosis, or pain out of proportion*
nursing: Remove dressing; irrigate and clean wound
Mark surrounding erythema; document drainage and odor
Obtain wound culture before antibiotics if purulent
Pain assessment and analgesia; IV access if systemic* if indicated
keyOrders: Wound exploration for foreign body, depth, and tendon/joint involvement
Bedside US for abscess or retained radiolucent foreign body
IV access for IV antibiotics if systemic infection* if indicated
labs: CMP, CRP, lactate if systemic/septic* if indicated
Blood cultures x2 if febrile/toxic* if indicated
Glucose/HbA1c context in diabetics* if indicated
imaging: Plain film for radiopaque foreign body or osteomyelitis/gas* if indicated
Bedside US for abscess or radiolucent foreign body
CT/MRI if deep infection, osteomyelitis, or necrotizing concern* if indicated
meds: Cephalexin 500 mg PO QID or amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg PO BID (bite/contaminated)
MRSA risk: TMP-SMX DS BID or doxycycline 100 mg BID; add coverage as needed
Severe/IV: cefazolin 1-2 g IV q8h or vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam for broad coverage
Tetanus: Tdap +/- TIG per immunization status; analgesia for pain
reassess: Recheck vitals and erythema margins after intervention
Confirm adequate drainage/debridement and foreign body removal
Verify tetanus prophylaxis given and tolerated
disposition: Discharge localized wound infection on oral antibiotics and wound care
Observation/IV antibiotics for moderate infection or comorbidities
Transfer septic, necrotizing, deep, or osteomyelitis cases for surgery/admission
discharge: Wound care instructions, dressing changes, keep clean and dry
Complete antibiotic course; elevate if extremity
Return for fever, spreading redness, increasing pain, drainage, or red streaking
followup: Wound recheck in 48-72 hours
Surgical/specialty follow-up for surgical site infections
Primary care for suture/staple removal and healing assessment

redFlags: Crepitus, bullae, dusky skin, pain out of proportion (necrotizing fasciitis/gas gangrene)
Sepsis: hypotension, tachycardia, altered mentation, elevated lactate
Exposed bone/tendon/joint or deep space involvement
Failure to improve or rapid progression despite antibiotics
compliance: EMTALA MSE to exclude necrotizing infection, deep-space involvement, and sepsis before disposition
Necrotizing or deep infections require emergent surgery; stabilize and transfer to surgical facility via appropriate-transfer protocol
Provide tetanus prophylaxis per CDC/Texas standards and document
Report animal/human bite wounds per Texas rabies/animal-bite reporting requirements; report suspected abuse-related wounds

name: Epistaxis
summary: Anterior (Kiesselbach) vs posterior bleed; assess volume, anticoagulation, and airway/hemodynamic risk.

triage: Have patient sit upright, lean forward, pinch soft nares x10-15 min
Quantify blood loss, duration, recurrence, trauma
Screen anticoagulant/antiplatelet use and bleeding disorders
Vitals; assess for hypovolemia/orthostasis
nursing: Apply continuous firm nasal compression; provide emesis basin
Oximetry; recheck BP
Prepare nasal speculum, headlamp, suction, cautery/packing kit
*IV access if active or high-volume bleed if indicated
keyOrders: *IV access and fluids if hemodynamically unstable if indicated
*Type & screen if significant blood loss if indicated
Treat hypertension only after bleeding controlled
labs: *PT/INR/PTT if on anticoagulant or liver disease if indicated
*Type & screen if transfusion possible if indicated
imaging: Usually none for atraumatic epistaxis
*CT face/sinus if trauma or suspected mass if indicated
meds: Oxymetazoline 0.05% 2 sprays/pledgets topical
Lidocaine 1-2% with epinephrine topical for anesthesia
Silver nitrate cautery to visualized anterior source
*Tranexamic acid 500 mg topical (soaked pledget) if indicated
*Reverse warfarin with vitamin K/PCC if life-threatening bleed if indicated
reassess: Inspect for cessation after vasoconstriction/compression
If continues anteriorly: cautery then anterior packing
If posterior/uncontrolled: posterior balloon pack + monitor
Recheck vitals and Hgb trend if significant loss
disposition: Discharge most controlled anterior bleeds after observation
Admit/observe posterior packs (risk of hypoxia/dysrhythmia)
Transfer to ENT/higher level for uncontrolled or posterior bleed needing intervention
discharge: Nasal saline gel, humidification, avoid nose-picking/blowing
Resume anticoagulation per shared decision once stable
Return for rebleed, lightheadedness, or pack dislodgement
followup: ENT follow-up in 24-72 h if packed
PCP for BP and anticoagulation management
*Hematology if recurrent unexplained bleeding if indicated

redFlags: Hemodynamic instability or large-volume blood loss
Posterior bleed not controlled with anterior measures
Airway compromise or recurrent bleeding on anticoagulation
Suspected nasal mass/juvenile angiofibroma
compliance: EMTALA MSE on all comers; stabilize bleeding before any transfer
Posterior packs and unstable bleeds typically require appropriate transfer to ENT-capable facility with provider-to-provider report
Document anticoagulation reversal decisions and observation-vs-transfer rationale
FSED must arrange qualified transport (ALS) for actively bleeding/hypotensive patients

name: Ear Pain / Otitis
summary: Differentiate AOM, otitis externa, and referred otalgia; exclude mastoiditis and malignant otitis externa.

triage: Onset, fever, discharge, water exposure, hearing change
Diabetes/immunocompromise (malignant OE risk)
Vitals including temperature
Assess for trismus or dental source of referred pain
nursing: Position for comfort; pain assessment
Prepare otoscope; gentle external canal cleaning if discharge
*Obtain temperature trend if febrile if indicated
keyOrders: Otoscopic exam of both ears with pneumatic insufflation if available
*Wick placement for swollen canal in OE if indicated
labs: *CBC/CRP if toxic or suspected mastoiditis if indicated
*Glucose/HbA1c if suspected malignant otitis externa if indicated
imaging: None routinely
*CT temporal bone if mastoiditis or malignant OE suspected if indicated
meds: AOM: amoxicillin 875-1000 mg PO BID (or high-dose 80-90 mg/kg/day basis)
PCN allergy: cefdinir 300 mg PO BID or azithromycin
Otitis externa: ofloxacin 0.3% or ciprofloxacin-dexamethasone otic drops
Analgesia: ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO and acetaminophen 1000 mg PO
*Topical antipyrine-benzocaine otic for analgesia if TM intact if indicated
reassess: Confirm pain control and ability to tolerate PO
Reassess canal patency after wick if OE
Watchful-waiting option for mild AOM in reliable adults
disposition: Discharge nearly all uncomplicated cases
Admit/transfer mastoiditis, malignant OE, or toxic appearance
ENT consult for complicated/refractory disease
discharge: Keep ear dry; complete antibiotic course
Analgesia schedule and expected 48-72 h improvement
Return for high fever, postauricular swelling, vertigo, facial weakness
followup: PCP in 1-2 weeks; recheck TM if symptoms persist
ENT for recurrent AOM or persistent effusion/hearing loss

redFlags: Postauricular erythema/swelling with protruding ear (mastoiditis)
Severe pain with granulation tissue in diabetic/immunocompromised (malignant OE)
Facial nerve palsy, vertigo, or meningeal signs
Toxic appearance or failure of outpatient therapy
compliance: EMTALA MSE required even for low-acuity complaints
Transfer mastoiditis/malignant OE to facility with ENT/IV antibiotic capability via appropriate transfer
Document antibiotic stewardship rationale (treat vs observe)

name: Pharyngitis / Sore Throat
summary: Mostly viral; risk-stratify GAS with Centor; exclude epiglottitis, peritonsillar abscess, and deep-space infection.

triage: Fever, exudate, cough, duration; Centor/McIsaac score
Drooling, muffled voice, stridor, trismus (airway red flags)
Vitals; assess hydration and ability to swallow
nursing: Position upright if any airway concern; do NOT force exam if stridor
Pain/hydration assessment
*IV access if dehydrated or toxic if indicated
keyOrders: Oropharyngeal exam (defer aggressive exam if epiglottitis suspected)
*Rapid strep antigen +/- throat culture if Centor >=2 if indicated
*IV fluids for poor PO intake if indicated
imaging: None routinely
*Lateral neck soft-tissue X-ray if epiglottitis suspected if indicated
*CT neck for deep-space infection if indicated
meds: GAS: penicillin V 500 mg PO BID-TID x10 days or amoxicillin 500 mg PO BID
PCN allergy: azithromycin 500 mg day1 then 250 mg or cephalexin
Analgesia: ibuprofen 600 mg PO and acetaminophen 1000 mg PO
*Single dose dexamethasone 10 mg PO/IM for severe odynophagia if indicated
*IV fluids (NS 1 L) for dehydration if indicated
reassess: Confirm tolerating PO and adequate analgesia
Reassess airway if any stridor/drooling developed
Avoid antibiotics for low-probability viral pharyngitis
disposition: Discharge uncomplicated cases
Admit/transfer suspected epiglottitis or deep-space infection
Urgent ENT/anesthesia for airway threat
discharge: Hydration, salt-water gargle, lozenges, analgesia schedule
Complete antibiotics if prescribed; no return to work/school 24 h after abx if GAS
Return for drooling, voice change, neck swelling, difficulty breathing
followup: PCP if no improvement in 3-5 days
*ENT if recurrent tonsillitis if indicated

redFlags: Stridor, drooling, tripod posture, or muffled 'hot potato' voice
Trismus or unilateral swelling (peritonsillar/deep-space abscess)
Rapidly progressive neck swelling/crepitus
Toxic appearance or inability to manage secretions
compliance: EMTALA MSE on all; never delay screening for low-acuity triage assumptions
Suspected epiglottitis/deep-space infection require stabilization and appropriate transfer with airway-capable team
Document antibiotic stewardship and Centor-based decision

name: Dental Pain / Abscess
summary: Odontogenic pain vs localized abscess vs spreading facial/deep-space infection (Ludwig angina).

triage: Onset, swelling, trismus, fever, dysphagia
Floor-of-mouth elevation or tongue swelling (Ludwig)
Vitals; assess airway and hydration
nursing: Position upright if facial swelling/airway concern
Pain assessment; prepare for I&D if fluctuant
*IV access if systemic infection/dehydration if indicated
keyOrders: Intraoral/extraoral exam; assess for fluctuance and floor of mouth
*Bedside I&D of fluctuant intraoral abscess if indicated
*IV fluids/antibiotics if spreading infection if indicated
labs: *Glucose if diabetic with severe infection if indicated
imaging: *Panorex/bedside if available for localization if indicated
*CT face/neck with contrast for deep-space/Ludwig if indicated
meds: Analgesia: ibuprofen 600 mg PO + acetaminophen 1000 mg PO scheduled
Dental block: bupivacaine 0.5% supraperiosteal/inferior alveolar
Abscess: amoxicillin 875 mg PO BID or amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg BID
PCN allergy: clindamycin 300-450 mg PO QID
*IV ampicillin-sulbactam 3 g for severe/spreading infection if indicated
reassess: Confirm analgesia and drainage if I&D performed
Reassess airway and swelling progression
Avoid antibiotics for simple pulpitis without swelling (stewardship)
disposition: Discharge localized infection/pain with dental follow-up
Admit/transfer Ludwig angina, deep-space infection, airway threat
OMFS/ENT for operative drainage as needed
discharge: Warm salt-water rinses, soft diet, scheduled analgesia
Complete antibiotics if prescribed; urgent dental referral
Return for facial swelling, trismus, difficulty swallowing/breathing
followup: Dentist/OMFS within 24-72 h for definitive care (extraction/root canal)
PCP if diabetic with infection

redFlags: Floor-of-mouth swelling, tongue elevation, drooling (Ludwig angina)
Trismus, periorbital swelling, or rapidly spreading cellulitis
Airway compromise or toxic appearance
Crepitus or signs of necrotizing infection
compliance: EMTALA MSE required; dental pain is a valid ED presentation
Ludwig angina/deep-space infection require stabilization and appropriate transfer to OMFS/airway-capable facility
Document opioid stewardship and antibiotic indication clearly

name: Eye Pain
summary: Differentiate surface (abrasion/keratitis) from sight-threatening causes (acute angle-closure glaucoma, optic neuritis, orbital cellulitis).

triage: Visual acuity each eye early; photophobia, halos, trauma, contact lens use
Associated headache, nausea, vision loss
Vitals; check for proptosis/limited movement
nursing: Obtain and document visual acuity both eyes (with correction)
Prepare slit lamp, fluorescein, tonometer
*Topical anesthetic for exam (not for discharge) if indicated
keyOrders: Visual acuity, pupils, EOM, slit-lamp with fluorescein
labs: *ESR/CRP if giant cell arteritis suspected if indicated
imaging: *CT orbits if trauma, foreign body, or orbital cellulitis if indicated
*MRI if optic neuritis suspected if indicated
meds: Corneal abrasion: erythromycin ophthalmic ointment QID; ciprofloxacin drops if contact-lens related
Analgesia: oral ibuprofen/acetaminophen; *cycloplegic (cyclopentolate 1%) for ciliary spasm if indicated
Acute angle-closure: timolol 0.5%, apraclonidine, pilocarpine 1-2%, + acetazolamide 500 mg IV/PO
*IV antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftriaxone) for orbital cellulitis if indicated
reassess: Recheck IOP after glaucoma treatment
Confirm acuity stable/improving
Never prescribe topical anesthetics for home use
disposition: Discharge simple abrasion/conjunctival causes
Emergent ophthalmology for angle-closure, orbital cellulitis, optic neuritis
Transfer if no ophthalmology coverage and vision threatened
discharge: No contact lens until cleared; analgesia plan
Strict return for worsening vision, severe pain, halos
Antibiotic ointment instructions
followup: Ophthalmology 24-48 h for abrasions (especially contact-lens wearers)
Urgent ophthalmology for any vision-threatening diagnosis

redFlags: Acute vision loss, halos around lights, fixed mid-dilated pupil, hard eye (angle-closure)
Proptosis, painful/limited EOM, fever (orbital cellulitis)
Hyphema, hypopyon, or penetrating injury
New afferent pupillary defect
compliance: EMTALA MSE with documented visual acuity as a vital sign
Sight-threatening conditions without on-site ophthalmology require expedited appropriate transfer
Document IOP and time-sensitive interventions; FSED should pre-arrange ophthalmology transfer pathway

name: Red Eye
summary: Distinguish benign conjunctivitis/subconjunctival hemorrhage from keratitis, iritis, glaucoma, or scleritis.

triage: Visual acuity; pain, photophobia, discharge, contact lens use
Unilateral vs bilateral; trauma or chemical exposure
Vitals; identify danger signs (pain, vision loss, photophobia)
nursing: Document visual acuity both eyes
Prepare slit lamp, fluorescein, tonometer
Contact precautions for suspected viral conjunctivitis
keyOrders: Slit-lamp with fluorescein; evaluate cornea and anterior chamber
Check pupils and IOP if pain/photophobia present
*Evert lids to inspect for foreign body if indicated
labs: *Conjunctival culture if hyperacute/gonococcal suspected if indicated
imaging: None routinely
meds: Bacterial conjunctivitis: erythromycin ointment or polymyxin-trimethoprim drops; ciprofloxacin if contact lens
Viral/allergic: artificial tears, cool compresses; *olopatadine 0.1% for allergic if indicated
Iritis: cycloplegic + topical steroid only with ophthalmology guidance
*Ceftriaxone 1 g IM + topical for gonococcal conjunctivitis if indicated
reassess: Confirm no corneal uptake/anterior chamber inflammation
Reassess if pain or vision worsens
Avoid topical steroids without slit-lamp/ophthalmology input
disposition: Discharge benign conjunctivitis/subconjunctival hemorrhage
Ophthalmology referral for keratitis, iritis, scleritis, or vision loss
Transfer vision-threatening cases without ophthalmology coverage
discharge: Hand hygiene, avoid sharing towels; no contacts until resolved
Antibiotic drop instructions if prescribed
Return for pain, vision change, photophobia, or no improvement in 48-72 h
followup: PCP/ophthalmology in 1 week if not resolving
Urgent ophthalmology for any danger-sign red eye

redFlags: Decreased visual acuity or severe pain
Photophobia, ciliary flush, or fixed pupil (iritis/glaucoma)
Corneal infiltrate/ulcer (especially contact lens wearer)
Hyperacute purulent discharge (gonococcal, corneal perforation risk)
compliance: EMTALA MSE with documented acuity; differentiate benign vs emergent
Suspected gonococcal/chlamydial conjunctivitis is a reportable STI in Texas; report per DSHS and treat partners
Vision-threatening red eye without coverage warrants appropriate transfer

name: Acute Vision Loss
summary: Time-critical: CRAO, retinal detachment, GCA, optic neuritis, or vision loss as stroke/TIA presentation.

triage: Immediate visual acuity each eye; onset, painless vs painful, monocular vs binocular
Associated neuro deficits, headache, jaw claudication (GCA)
Vitals; treat as stroke equivalent if binocular field loss
Note time of onset (thrombolytic/intervention window)
nursing: Document acuity, confrontation fields, pupils (APD)
Cardiac monitor and IV access
Activate stroke pathway if neuro signs present
Prepare for emergent ophthalmology contact
keyOrders: Visual acuity, pupils for APD, fundoscopy, IOP
*Stroke alert/neuro consult if binocular or neuro deficits if indicated
*Digital ocular massage for suspected CRAO if indicated
labs: *ESR, CRP, platelets STAT if GCA suspected if indicated
*CBC, coags if stroke pathway if indicated
imaging: *CT/CTA head & neck (or MRI) if stroke/neuro cause if indicated
*Bedside ocular ultrasound for detachment/CRAO if indicated
meds: GCA suspected: methylprednisolone 1 g IV (or prednisone 60-80 mg) to preserve vision
Optic neuritis: methylprednisolone 1 g IV per ophthalmology/neurology
Stroke presentation: manage per stroke protocol (tPA/thrombectomy eligibility)
*CRAO measures: ocular massage, acetazolamide 500 mg IV, anterior chamber paracentesis by ophthalmology if indicated
reassess: Serial acuity and neuro checks
Confirm time-critical consult contact made
Reassess after any acute intervention
disposition: Emergent ophthalmology/neurology involvement for all
Transfer to stroke/ophthalmology-capable center as indicated
Admit per underlying diagnosis
discharge: Rarely discharged from FSED; only after specialist clearance
If discharged: strict return precautions and confirmed urgent follow-up
followup: Same-day/next-day ophthalmology
Neurology/stroke clinic if vascular etiology
Rheumatology if GCA confirmed

redFlags: Painless monocular loss (CRAO/retinal detachment) - time-critical
Headache/jaw claudication/scalp tenderness with vision loss (GCA)
Binocular field loss or other neuro deficits (stroke)
Afferent pupillary defect, curtain/flashes/floaters
compliance: EMTALA MSE prioritized; acute vision loss is high-acuity and stabilization-first
FSED almost always requires appropriate transfer to ophthalmology/stroke-capable hospital; arrange ALS transport without delay
Document onset time, acuity, and consult/transfer timeline carefully

name: Ocular Foreign Body
summary: Surface foreign body vs intraocular/penetrating injury; exclude globe rupture, especially after high-velocity mechanism.

triage: Mechanism (grinding/hammering = high velocity), material, time
Visual acuity each eye; pain, tearing
Vitals; suspect globe rupture if high-velocity metal-on-metal
nursing: Document visual acuity
Do NOT manipulate or apply pressure if rupture suspected; apply rigid shield
Prepare slit lamp, fluorescein, irrigation supplies
keyOrders: Slit-lamp exam with fluorescein; check Seidel sign
Evert upper lid to inspect for retained FB
*Place protective eye shield if globe rupture suspected if indicated
imaging: *CT orbit (thin-cut) if intraocular FB or rupture suspected if indicated
Avoid MRI if metallic FB possible
meds: Topical proparacaine 0.5% for exam/removal
After removal: erythromycin ointment QID or fluoroquinolone drops (contact lens wearers)
*Cycloplegic (cyclopentolate 1%) for photophobia/ciliary spasm if indicated
*Tetanus prophylaxis (Tdap) if indicated; *IV antibiotics + antiemetic, NPO for open globe if indicated
reassess: Recheck acuity after removal; assess for residual rust ring
Confirm no Seidel sign / globe integrity
Avoid removal attempts if penetrating injury
disposition: Discharge uncomplicated surface FB after removal
Emergent ophthalmology and transfer for suspected open globe/intraocular FB
Ophthalmology referral for rust ring or central FB
discharge: Antibiotic ointment, no eye rubbing, eye protection at work
Return for worsening pain, vision change, discharge
Counsel on protective eyewear
followup: Ophthalmology 24-48 h for rust ring removal or central/deep FB
PCP/occupational health as appropriate

redFlags: Positive Seidel sign or teardrop/peaked pupil (globe rupture)
High-velocity mechanism (grinding/hammering) with possible intraocular FB
Marked vision loss, hyphema, or shallow anterior chamber
Severe pain disproportionate to exam
compliance: EMTALA MSE with documented acuity before any procedure
Suspected open globe: shield, NPO, IV antibiotics, antiemetics, and appropriate transfer to ophthalmology-capable facility
Document mechanism, tetanus status, and protective-eyewear counseling; consider workplace injury reporting

name: Ear / Nose Foreign Body
summary: Usually benign; urgent for button battery, paired magnets, or organic FB with infection risk.

triage: Object type, side, duration, prior removal attempts
Identify button battery or paired magnets (time-critical)
Assess for unilateral nasal discharge (chronic FB) or bleeding
nursing: Position and immobilize patient (especially peds)
Prepare otoscope/nasal speculum, alligator forceps, suction, balloon catheter
Reassure to reduce movement during removal
keyOrders: *Positive-pressure ('mother's kiss') for nasal FB if indicated
*Mineral oil/lidocaine to kill live insect before ear removal if indicated
Expedite removal/transfer if button battery or magnets
imaging: *Plain X-ray to confirm/locate radiopaque FB or battery if indicated
meds: *Topical lidocaine or mineral oil to immobilize insect in ear if indicated
*Topical anesthetic/vasoconstrictor (oxymetazoline + lidocaine) for nasal FB if indicated
*Antibiotic otic drops if canal trauma/otitis after removal if indicated
*Anxiolysis/procedural sedation in select agitated patients if indicated
reassess: Confirm complete removal and inspect for trauma/second FB
Reassess canal/nasal mucosa for injury
Limit attempts to avoid pushing FB deeper - know when to refer
disposition: Discharge after successful uncomplicated removal
Urgent ENT/transfer for button battery, magnets, or failed removal
ENT for deeply impacted or TM-adjacent objects
discharge: Watch for discharge, pain, or bleeding
Tissue-care instructions if mucosal trauma
Return if symptoms recur (possible retained FB)
followup: ENT follow-up for incomplete removal or tissue injury
PCP as needed

redFlags: Button battery in ear or nose (liquefaction necrosis - emergent removal)
Paired magnets (septal/mucosal pressure necrosis)
Uncontrolled bleeding or signs of perforation
Posterior/deep impaction risking aspiration or canal injury
compliance: EMTALA MSE prior to removal attempts
Button battery/magnets require emergent ENT - stabilize and arrange appropriate transfer if no ENT on site
Consider non-accidental injury/neglect screening in vulnerable patients and report suspected abuse per Texas law

name: Peritonsillar Abscess
summary: Unilateral tonsillar swelling with uvular deviation and trismus; assess airway and distinguish from deep-space infection.

triage: Severe unilateral sore throat, trismus, muffled voice, drooling
Assess airway patency and ability to handle secretions
Vitals; fever and dehydration assessment
nursing: Upright positioning; airway monitoring
IV access; pain and hydration assessment
Prepare drainage tray (needle aspiration/I&D), headlamp, suction
keyOrders: Oropharyngeal exam: uvular deviation, peritonsillar fullness, trismus
*Needle aspiration or I&D of fluctuant abscess if indicated
*Bedside intraoral ultrasound to confirm/guide drainage if indicated
imaging: *Intraoral or transcutaneous ultrasound to differentiate abscess vs cellulitis if indicated
*CT neck with contrast if deep-space extension or diagnosis unclear if indicated
meds: Ampicillin-sulbactam 3 g IV (or clindamycin 600-900 mg IV if PCN allergy)
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV for swelling/pain
Analgesia: IV ketorolac 15-30 mg and/or opioids; acetaminophen
Local anesthesia: lidocaine 1% + topical for drainage
*IV fluids (NS 1-2 L) for dehydration if indicated
reassess: Reassess airway and trismus after drainage/steroids
Confirm tolerating secretions/PO before any discharge
Recheck pain and swelling response
disposition: Discharge after successful drainage if tolerating PO and stable airway
Admit/transfer if airway concern, failed drainage, deep-space extension, or unable to tolerate PO
ENT consult/transfer for drainage if not performed on site
discharge: Complete oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg BID) and analgesia
Hydration and soft diet; warm salt-water rinses
Return immediately for difficulty breathing, increasing swelling, or inability to swallow
followup: ENT within 24-48 h
PCP as needed; discuss tonsillectomy if recurrent

redFlags: Stridor, drooling, or respiratory distress (airway compromise)
Inability to handle secretions or tolerate PO
Extension to parapharyngeal/retropharyngeal space
Crepitus or signs of necrotizing/spreading infection
compliance: EMTALA MSE and airway stabilization before any transfer
If no ENT/drainage capability or airway risk, perform appropriate transfer with airway-capable transport
Document drainage procedure, airway assessment, and observation-vs-transfer decision

name: Sinusitis
summary: Mostly viral; identify bacterial sinusitis criteria and exclude orbital/intracranial complications.

triage: Duration, purulent discharge, facial pain/pressure, fever
Double-sickening pattern; >10 days without improvement
Screen for orbital (vision/eye swelling) and CNS red flags
Vitals including temperature
nursing: Comfort positioning; pain assessment
*Temperature trend if febrile if indicated
*Eye exam/acuity if periorbital involvement if indicated
keyOrders: HEENT exam including sinus palpation, nasal mucosa, eyes
Cranial nerve and visual acuity check if any orbital signs
*IV access if toxic or complication suspected if indicated
imaging: Not indicated for uncomplicated acute sinusitis
*CT sinus/orbits/brain with contrast if orbital or intracranial complication suspected if indicated
meds: Symptomatic: intranasal saline, intranasal fluticasone, oxymetazoline (<=3 days)
Analgesia/antipyretic: ibuprofen 600 mg PO, acetaminophen 1000 mg PO
Bacterial criteria: amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg PO BID x5-7 days
PCN allergy: doxycycline 100 mg PO BID or levofloxacin
*IV ceftriaxone + vancomycin if orbital/intracranial complication if indicated
reassess: Confirm no orbital/neuro findings
Reassess pain control and hydration
Apply watchful-waiting for likely viral cases (stewardship)
disposition: Discharge nearly all uncomplicated cases
Admit/transfer orbital cellulitis, abscess, or intracranial extension
ENT/ophthalmology/neurosurgery for complications
discharge: Saline irrigation, nasal steroids, hydration, analgesia
Antibiotics only if bacterial criteria met; complete course
Return for vision change, eye swelling, severe headache, neck stiffness, confusion
followup: PCP in 7-10 days if not improving
*ENT for recurrent/chronic sinusitis if indicated

redFlags: Periorbital/orbital swelling, proptosis, painful or limited EOM, vision change (orbital cellulitis)
Severe headache, altered mental status, neck stiffness, seizure (intracranial spread)
High fever/toxic appearance
Frontal swelling (Pott puffy tumor)
compliance: EMTALA MSE on all; differentiate uncomplicated from complicated sinusitis
Orbital/intracranial complications require stabilization and appropriate transfer to facility with ENT/neurosurgery/ophthalmology
Document antibiotic stewardship rationale (viral vs bacterial criteria)

name: Anaphylaxis / Allergic Reaction
summary: IgE-mediated multisystem hypersensitivity ranging from urticaria to airway/cardiovascular collapse; distinguish from isolated urticaria and angioedema.

triage: Rapid airway/breathing/circulation assessment; pull to high-acuity bed immediately
Identify trigger and time of exposure (food, sting, drug, latex)
Grade severity: skin-only vs respiratory/GI/CV involvement
Vitals with continuous SpO2; note stridor, wheeze, hypotension
Ask about prior anaphylaxis and current beta-blocker/ACE-I use
nursing: Place supine with legs elevated unless dyspneic; avoid sudden upright posture
Apply continuous cardiac monitor and pulse oximetry
Establish 2 large-bore IVs
Prepare epinephrine and airway/intubation equipment at bedside
Remove ongoing trigger (stinger, IV drug, food)
keyOrders: IM epinephrine FIRST-LINE without delay for systemic involvement
Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, frequent BP
IV access x2 with crystalloid bolus ready
NPO; prepare for possible difficult airway
*Epinephrine infusion if indicated for refractory shock
labs: Usually clinical diagnosis; labs not required to treat
*Serum tryptase within 1-2 hr if indicated (diagnostic confirmation)
*Basic metabolic panel if indicated with prolonged shock
imaging: None routinely indicated
*CXR if indicated for persistent wheeze or aspiration concern
*Soft-tissue neck imaging deferred if airway unstable
meds: Epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg IM (1 mg/mL) anterolateral thigh, repeat q5-15 min
Normal saline 1-2 L IV bolus for hypotension (repeat to 20-30 mL/kg)
Diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV (adjunct, not primary)
Famotidine 20 mg IV (H2 adjunct)
Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV (prevent biphasic; adjunct)
Albuterol 2.5-5 mg nebulized for bronchospasm
Glucagon 1-5 mg IV for epinephrine-refractory patients on beta-blockers
reassess: Reassess airway, BP, and breathing q5-15 min after each epinephrine dose
Escalate to epinephrine infusion if no response to repeat IM doses
Watch for biphasic reaction over observation period
Reevaluate need for early intubation if progressive stridor/edema
disposition: Observe 4-6 hr minimum after symptom resolution (longer if severe/refractory)
Admit/transfer if required repeat epinephrine, infusion, or airway intervention
Discharge skin-only reactions after observation if stable
ICU-level transfer for refractory shock or airway compromise
discharge: Prescribe 2 epinephrine auto-injectors and demonstrate use
5-day course non-sedating antihistamine +/- short steroid taper
Strict trigger avoidance instructions
Return precautions for recurrent or biphasic symptoms
followup: Allergy/immunology referral for trigger testing
PCP follow-up within 1 week
Consider medical-alert bracelet
Reinforce auto-injector carry and expiration checks

redFlags: Stridor, hoarseness, or progressive tongue/lip swelling (airway)
Hypotension or shock refractory to IM epinephrine
Hypoxia or severe bronchospasm unresponsive to bronchodilators
Patient on beta-blocker with poor epinephrine response
Biphasic reaction during observation
compliance: EMTALA: complete medical screening exam and stabilize airway/hemodynamics before any transfer
Refractory anaphylaxis or need for ICU/airway management warrants appropriate transfer with physician-to-physician handoff and copies of records
Document observation period rationale (FSED observation vs transfer decision) given biphasic risk
If reaction is to a medication administered at the FSED, document and report per facility adverse-event policy
Provide auto-injector prescription before discharge; FSEDs must ensure outpatient access plan

name: Heat Illness / Hyperthermia
summary: Spectrum from heat cramps/exhaustion to life-threatening heat stroke with CNS dysfunction and end-organ injury; distinguish from sepsis, thyroid storm, drug toxidromes.

triage: Obtain core temperature (rectal preferred) immediately
Assess mental status; altered sensorium suggests heat stroke
History of exertion, environmental exposure, anticholinergic/stimulant drugs
Vitals including SpO2; note tachycardia and hypotension
Flag temp >40C with CNS changes as critical
nursing: Begin active cooling immediately; remove clothing
Cardiac monitor, continuous core temp probe, SpO2
Establish IV access x2
Apply evaporative cooling (mist + fans) and ice packs to groin/axilla/neck
Insert Foley to monitor output and assess rhabdo/AKI
keyOrders: Active cooling to target core <39C, then stop to avoid overshoot
*Cold-water immersion if indicated for exertional heat stroke
labs: *TSH and drug screen if indicated to exclude mimics
imaging: 12-lead ECG for arrhythmia/ischemia
*CXR if indicated for aspiration/ARDS
*CT head if indicated for unexplained altered mentation
meds: Normal saline or LR 1-2 L IV bolus, titrate to perfusion/output
Benzodiazepine (lorazepam 1-2 mg IV) to control shivering during cooling
Acetaminophen/antipyretics NOT effective for environmental hyperthermia
Sodium bicarbonate per protocol if significant rhabdomyolysis/acidosis
*Dantrolene only if indicated for malignant hyperthermia/NMS mimic
reassess: Recheck core temp q5-10 min during active cooling
